channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

  4. MIT scientists develop a new way to grow artificial blood vessels  The Brighter Side of News

item.title: A teen spotted a rare pair of birds in D.C. Then came another surprise. - The Washington Post
item.description: A teen spotted a rare pair of birds in D.C. Then came another surprise.  The Washington Post

item.title: The Longest Journey Sunlight Ever Takes Happens Before It Leaves The Sun - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. The Longest Journey Sunlight Ever Takes Happens Before It Leaves The Sun  ScienceAlert
  2. Most people think the Sun is a vast ball of burning gas, but that familiar picture is quietly wrong: the Sun is plasma, a fourth state of matter — atoms torn into charged particles, the same stuff as lightning and neon  Space Daily
  3. Every second, the Sun converts about 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium and loses roughly 4 million tonnes of that mass outright as energy, and the light warming your face today left the core as gamma rays that took somewhere between 10,000 and 1  ScienceBlog.com
  4. Energy created in the Sun’s core can spend around 170,000 years bouncing through the radiative zone before reaching the outer layers — but once released as sunlight, it crosses 150 million kilometres to Earth in just over eight minutes  ScienceBlog.com
  5. You never see the Sun as it is, only as it was: its light takes eight minutes and twenty seconds to cross the 150 million kilometres to Earth — so if the Sun blinked out, we would bask on, unknowing, for eight more minutes  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: Mysterious 'little red dots' at the beginning time may finally have an explanation, thanks to newfound 'little blue companions' - Live Science
item.description:
1. Mysterious 'little red dots' at the beginning time may finally have an explanation, thanks to newfound 'little blue companions'  Live Science
  2. One idea, two cosmic mysteries—linking Little Red Dots and globular clusters  Phys.org
  3. Little red dot galaxies may solve a cosmic neutrino mystery  Yahoo
  4. Astronomers’ New Theory: Mysterious ‘Little Red Dots’ May Be Newborn Star Clusters  StudyFinds
  5. A mysterious “red dot” spotted in space: are we witnessing a cosmic birth?  Futura, le média qui explore le monde

item.title: In 1977, NASA launched two probes designed to last five years. Voyager 1 and 2 are now the only human-made objects in interstellar space, still operating, still transmitting, funded by a team of engineers some of whom have worked on the mission for nearly 5 - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 1977, NASA launched two probes designed to last five years. Voyager 1 and 2 are now the only human-made objects in interstellar space, still operating, still transmitting, funded by a team of engineers some of whom have worked on the mission for nearly 5  Space Daily
  2. Voyager 1 on track to reach one light-day from Earth  it-online.co.za
  3. NASA's Voyager 1 Radio Transmitter Is Weaker Than a Refrigerator Lightbulb. It Still Reaches Earth From 15 Billion Miles Away  19FortyFive
  4. Voyager 1 is still transmitting from more than 25 billion kilometres away, powered by less than 250 watts of decaying plutonium — a signal that now takes over 23 hours to reach Earth, carried by a radio no stronger than a refrigerator lightbulb  Make Tech Easier
  5. The engineers who built Voyager knew they would almost certainly be dead before it left the solar system, and they built it anyway, a gift from people who would never see where their work would go  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: Bee brains reveal how colonies divide work without central planning - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Bee brains reveal how colonies divide work without central planning  Phys.org
  2. Scientists studying bee brains found clues to how colonies coordinate complex behaviour without a central controller, relying instead on many small individual decisions  The Times of India
  3. Honey bees often split jobs by age, but shared food and social cues may help decide who nurses, who forages, and how the colony stays organised  The Times of India

item.description:

  3. Pit found by Quebecer confirmed to be a 390-million-years-old meteorite impact crater  CTV News
  4. Google Maps leads Quebec man to 390-million-year-old meteor crater  The Weather Network
  5. Giant pit discovered by hiker on Google Maps confirmed to be 390 million-year-old meteor impact crater  Live Science

item.title: The Universe Can Expand Faster Than Light Without Breaking Physics - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. The Universe Can Expand Faster Than Light Without Breaking Physics  SciTechDaily
  2. The observable universe is roughly 93 billion light-years across despite being only 13.8 billion years old, because space kept expanding while the oldest light was travelling toward us  Space Daily

item.title: Einstein’s “Greatest Blunder” May Be Breaking Cosmology - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. Einstein’s “Greatest Blunder” May Be Breaking Cosmology  SciTechDaily
  2. Does dark energy really exist? Our work identifies cracks in the foundations of today’s cosmological model  The Conversation
  3. We tend to think dark energy has always pushed the universe outward at the same steady rate, but a 2025 survey of 13.1 million galaxies found it appears to have been stronger for the first nine billion years, then began weakening around 4.5 billion years ago  Space Daily
